goal: README polish for trial: canonical positioning + lint/hub/context in quickstart and daily-commands

summary.title: README polish for trial: canonical positioning + lint/hub/context in quickstart and daily-commands
summary.what: Three targeted edits to README.md ahead of tomorrow's user trial. (1) Replace the drifting opening tagline with the spec §1 canonical positioning line, matching what AGENTS.md v11 already leads with. (2) Add `mainline context --current` and `mainline lint` to the 5-minute quickstart, plus a one-liner pointer to `mainline hub open` — none of these were in the quickstart even though all three are shipped surfaces. (3) Fix the Daily-commands table: split `mainline context` into the three retrieval modes (--current/--files/--query) with the right framing, and add the missing `mainline lint` and `mainline hub` rows.
summary.why: Trial users land on the README before any CLI command. The audit found the README quickstart matched a pre-Step-2 reality: no context-first step, no lint discoverability, hub absent, the context row described a legacy state-dump shape. The README is the front door; for a trial, the front door has to match the inside.
summary.user_goal: Trial users follow the README quickstart and end up running the full intent-first loop (context → edit → seal → lint → push) on first contact, without having to read AGENTS.md to discover lint or hub.

decisions.point: what to add to the quickstart
decisions.chose: context --current as a numbered step, lint as an optional numbered step, hub as a closing one-liner.
decisions.rationale: context is load-bearing for the product thesis — putting it inline as a step (not a footnote) signals 'this is part of the loop, not optional'. Lint is genuinely optional, so it's marked '(Optional)'. Hub is for a different audience (humans, not agents), so it's a closing pointer rather than a numbered step.

decisions.chose: Three rows for the three modes, not one row with a comma-separated mode list.
decisions.rationale: The modes have different audiences (--current = mid-flight agent; --files = file investigator; --query = semantic search). A single row hides the distinction; three rows let the reader scan and pick the one that matches their question.

decisions.chose: Skipped: rewriting the architecture diagram, deepening the FAQ, expanding the storage layout section.
decisions.rationale: All of those are accurate today. The trial bottleneck is the front door, not the deep dive.

rejected.alternative: Full README rewrite to match the spec roadmap section by section.
rejected.reason: Spec is internal alignment doc, README is product-facing. Not the same document.

rejected.alternative: Add a separate 'For agents' section with intent-first workflow.
rejected.reason: AGENTS.md is that section. Duplicating it in README invites them to drift apart.

rejected.alternative: Promote `mainline lint` into the seal flow's Step 4 (not optional).
rejected.reason: Lint is advisory; the seal flow already runs the soft-lint summary inline (PR #70). Making it required in the README would over-promise.

risks: Splitting `mainline context` into three rows in the table makes the table longer; readers scanning for 'context' may have to read three rows to find their use case. Acceptable: that's exactly the discoverability improvement we want.

anti_patterns.what: Letting the README's product positioning drift from AGENTS.md template's positioning
anti_patterns.why: Two front doors with two different one-liners confuse readers and give them two mental models to merge. Same line in both places, always.

followups: FAQ section: add 'When should I run mainline lint?' and 'Who is mainline hub for?' as canonical answers.
Architecture diagram: currently shows author/reviewer/merge/pin lanes; doesn't mention the intent-first read step. Worth a one-line addition under Author.
Once the LLM eval runner ships (Step 5 followup), README should link to the eval results doc.

architectural_claims: README and AGENTS.md template share the same canonical product-positioning line — duplicated content is intentional alignment, not drift.
The quickstart is the load-bearing onboarding surface for trial users; new shipped commands must be discoverable here, not just via --help.
behavioral_changes: Quickstart step count grows from 5 to 7 (added context-first read + optional lint).
Daily-commands table gains 4 rows (3 context modes + lint + hub) and replaces the misleading single-line context description.
